name: OS Command Injection
description: The application constructs OS commands using externally-influenced input without proper neutralization, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary commands.
detection_patterns: exec(), popen(), shell_exec() with unsanitized arguments
subprocess with shell=True and user input
String concatenation or interpolation used to build shell commands
remediation: Use parameterized APIs that avoid the shell. Use allowlists for permitted commands. Validate and sanitize all inputs used in command construction.

name: SQL Injection
description: The application constructs SQL queries using user-controlled input without proper neutralization, allowing attackers to modify query logic.
detection_patterns: f-strings or format strings containing SQL with user input
Direct insertion of request parameters into queries
Custom sanitize functions instead of parameterized queries
examples: query = 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = \'' + username + '\''
remediation: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements. Use ORM frameworks. Never concatenate user input directly into SQL.

name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
description: The application includes user-controllable input in web output without proper encoding, allowing execution of malicious scripts in other users' browsers.
detection_patterns: User input echoed directly in HTML without encoding
Template variables marked as 'safe' or using raw output
Reflected query parameters in page output
Stored user content rendered without sanitization
examples: render_template_string with user input marked safe
remediation: Encode output based on context (HTML, JS, URL, CSS). Use auto-escaping template engines. Implement Content-Security-Policy headers.

name: Buffer Overflow
description: The program copies input to a buffer without checking that the input size is within the buffer's bounds, potentially allowing code execution.
detection_patterns: read() or recv() with size larger than buffer
Missing bounds checks on array indices
remediation: Use bounded functions (strncpy, snprintf, fgets). Validate buffer sizes before copy operations. Use safe string libraries.

name: Uncontrolled Format String
description: The software uses a function that accepts a format string as an argument, but the format string originates from an external source.
detection_patterns: Format functions where first arg is user-controlled
remediation: Always use format specifiers: printf('%s', user_string). Never pass user-controlled strings as format arguments.

name: Use After Free
description: The program continues to use memory after it has been freed, leading to undefined behavior and potential code execution.
detection_patterns: Pointer used after free() without being set to NULL
Array entries not cleared after freeing pointed-to memory
Freed objects still referenced through aliases
Conditional free paths where pointer may still be used
remediation: Set pointers to NULL after freeing. Clear container entries. Use RAII in C++. Consider using smart pointers.

name: Integer Overflow
description: An integer calculation can produce a value that is outside the range of the integer type, leading to unexpected wrapping behavior.
detection_patterns: Multiplication of integers without overflow checking
Arithmetic used for buffer size calculations
Signed integer used where unsigned expected
No validation of integer inputs before arithmetic
examples: int result = a * b; /* no overflow check */
remediation: Use safe integer arithmetic libraries. Check for overflow before performing arithmetic. Use wider types for intermediate calculations.

name: Race Condition (TOCTOU)
description: The program checks a condition and then acts on it, but the condition can change between the check and the action, leading to security issues.
detection_patterns: File existence checks followed by file operations
Balance or permission checks followed by modifications without locking
examples: Check permission then modify without lock
remediation: Use atomic operations or proper locking mechanisms. Implement mutex/semaphore protection for shared resources.

name: Insecure Deserialization
description: The application deserializes untrusted data without verification, which can result in arbitrary code execution.
detection_patterns: unserialize() in PHP with user data
remediation: Never deserialize untrusted data. Use safe serialization formats (JSON). Implement integrity checks on serialized data.

name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: The application makes HTTP requests to a URL controlled by the attacker, allowing access to internal services.
detection_patterns: No URL allowlist or scheme validation
remediation: Implement URL allowlists. Validate URL schemes (reject file://, gopher://). Block requests to internal/private IP ranges.

name: Path Traversal
description: The application uses user input to construct file paths without proper validation, allowing access to files outside intended directories.
detection_patterns: File paths constructed with user input
send_file() or readfile() with user-controlled paths
remediation: Canonicalize paths and verify they stay within intended directory. Use allowlists for permitted files. Strip or reject path traversal sequences.

name: Hardcoded Credentials
description: The application contains hardcoded passwords, API keys, or cryptographic keys in source code.
detection_patterns: Password strings assigned in source code
API keys or tokens as string literals
Default admin credentials in initialization code
remediation: Use environment variables or secure vaults for secrets. Implement key management systems. Never commit credentials to source control.

name: Use of Broken Cryptographic Algorithm
description: The application uses a cryptographic algorithm that is known to be weak or broken.
remediation: Use bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 for password hashing. Use AES-256-GCM for encryption. Use cryptographically secure random IVs.

name: Improper Access Control
description: The application does not properly restrict access to resources, allowing unauthorized users to perform privileged operations.
detection_patterns: Missing authorization checks on sensitive endpoints
IDOR - direct object references without ownership validation
Missing CSRF tokens on state-changing operations
API endpoints returning data without permission checks
examples: get_note(note_id) without checking user owns it
DELETE endpoint with no auth check
Role check via client cookie instead of server session
remediation: Implement server-side authorization. Validate resource ownership. Use CSRF tokens. Never trust client-side role indicators.

name: Information Exposure
description: The application exposes sensitive information to unauthorized users through error messages, debug endpoints, or data leaks.
detection_patterns: Verbose error messages with stack traces
Credentials or tokens in log files
remediation: Disable debug mode in production. Implement proper error handling. Remove debug endpoints. Sanitize log output.

name: Code Injection
description: The application constructs code from user input and executes it, allowing arbitrary code execution.
detection_patterns: Dynamic code generation from user input
remediation: Never execute code derived from user input. Use allowlists for dynamic includes. Implement sandboxed execution environments if dynamic code is required.

name: XML External Entity (XXE)
description: The application parses XML input that can reference external entities, leading to data disclosure or SSRF.
detection_patterns: XML parsing without disabling external entities
DOM or SAX parsers with default configurations
remediation: Disable external entity processing. Use JSON instead of XML where possible. Configure parsers securely.

name: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
description: The application does not validate that state-changing requests originate from the application itself, allowing attackers to forge requests.
detection_patterns: POST endpoints without CSRF token validation
DELETE or UPDATE operations without origin validation
examples: <form method='POST'> with no hidden token field
remediation: Implement CSRF tokens on all state-changing requests. Use SameSite cookie attribute. Validate Origin/Referer headers.

name: Improper Restriction of Recursive Entity References
description: The application allows recursive or circular references in data structures, leading to resource exhaustion.
detection_patterns: Recursive data processing without depth limits
No maximum recursion depth on user-controlled structures
examples: Deeply nested JSON without depth limit
remediation: Set maximum recursion depth. Limit entity expansion. Validate input structure depth.

name: Incorrect Permission Assignment
description: The application creates files or resources with permissions that are more permissive than necessary.
detection_patterns: Log files with sensitive info and broad permissions
remediation: Apply principle of least privilege. Set restrictive file permissions. Use specific CORS origins.
